title: Why some apologies are worse than silence
summary: Argue that certain kinds of apologies cause more harm than saying nothing at all.
topic: Why some apologies are worse than silence
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) arguing that certain types of apologies — such as the non-apology ('I'm sorry you feel that way'), the over-qualified apology, or the apology that shifts blame — are more damaging than staying silent. Use a cleft sentence, a concession ('Granted, …'), and at least one passive construction. Include at least one concrete example (e.g. a corporate PR apology, a political statement, or an interpersonal scenario). Take a clear side.
hintStarters: The most corrosive apology is not the one that is withheld but the one that…
What a non-apology communicates is not remorse but a refusal to…
Granted, any attempt to repair a relationship is preferable to…
There is a particular kind of statement — grammatically an apology, functionally an…

được nhìn nhận là
trách nhiệm giải trình

thu nhỏ / hạ thấp (tầm quan trọng)
lời xin lỗi kèm quá nhiều điều kiện

xứng đáng được nhận lời xin lỗi

làm trầm trọng thêm một tổn thương
khôi phục niềm tin
hành vi xúc phạm lần thứ hai
bị bác bỏ như là
lời buộc tội ẩn
nhận trách nhiệm về
bị chuyển hướng sang

sampleText:
There is a particular kind of statement — grammatically an apology, functionally an insult — that has become standard in political speeches and corporate press releases. It runs roughly as follows: 'I am sorry if anyone was offended.' No harm is named. No responsibility is accepted. The passive construction does its work invisibly, and the person who was hurt is left holding both the injury and the implication that they may have imagined it.

What a statement like 'I'm sorry if anyone was offended' communicates is not remorse but a refusal to acknowledge that anything actually happened. The word 'if' is doing extraordinary damage in that sentence: it reframes the harm as a possibility rather than a fact, and it deflects responsibility onto the perception of the injured person. This is not an apology — it is an implicit accusation. And an implicit accusation, delivered in the form of an apology, compounds the original injury rather than addressing it.

Granted, any attempt to reach out after a conflict is preferable to permanent silence in many cases. A clumsy apology can still signal that a relationship has value, and there are situations where an imperfect attempt at repair matters more than whether the wording is precisely right. Not everyone has the emotional vocabulary to express remorse clearly, and it would be harsh to demand precision from someone doing their best with limited tools.

Even so, the over-qualified apology — 'I'm sorry, but you did provoke me', or 'I apologise if my words were taken out of context' — is not simply imprecise. It shifts blame onto the recipient in the very act of pretending to take responsibility. Research in interpersonal communication consistently finds that conditional apologies are perceived as worse than no apology at all by the people who receive them: they feel not only unrepaired but actively dismissed. The injury is confirmed, and then immediately minimised.

What is being asked of a genuine apology is not elaborate language but a simple willingness to take ownership of a specific act. 'I said something that hurt you, and I regret it' costs nothing and restores the basic condition for trust: the acknowledgement that the other person's experience was real. A non-apology withholds precisely that acknowledgement, and in doing so it performs, in polished language, exactly the same erasure as saying nothing at all — except that it also requires the injured person to sit through it.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to expose hidden meaning
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a statement like … communicates is not remorse but a refusal to acknowledge' dùng cấu trúc 'What … communicates is …' để trì hoãn điều quan trọng nhất — 'a refusal to acknowledge' — cho đến cuối câu, nơi nó có sức nặng tối đa.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a statement like … communicates is not remorse but a refusal to acknowledge' uses a what-cleft to delay the key phrase until the end of the sentence, where it carries the most rhetorical weight.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to foreground who is affected
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'conditional apologies are perceived as worse than no apology at all by the people who receive them' dùng bị động để đặt 'conditional apologies' làm chủ ngữ, nhấn mạnh vào đối tượng bị đánh giá — lời xin lỗi — chứ không phải vào người đánh giá chúng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'conditional apologies are perceived as worse than no apology at all' uses the passive to place the apologies in subject position, focusing on what is being judged rather than on who is doing the judging.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một loại câu nói đặc biệt — về mặt ngữ pháp là lời xin lỗi, về mặt chức năng là sự xúc phạm — đã trở thành tiêu chuẩn trong các bài phát biểu chính trị và thông cáo báo chí doanh nghiệp. Nó chạy đại loại như sau: 'Tôi xin lỗi nếu ai đó cảm thấy bị xúc phạm.' Không có tổn hại nào được nêu tên. Không có trách nhiệm nào được chấp nhận. Cấu trúc bị động thực hiện công việc của nó một cách vô hình, và người bị tổn thương bị bỏ lại với cả tổn thương lẫn hàm ý rằng họ có thể đã tưởng tượng ra nó.

Điều mà một câu nói như 'Tôi xin lỗi nếu ai đó cảm thấy bị xúc phạm' truyền đạt không phải là sự hối hận mà là sự từ chối thừa nhận rằng có gì đó thực sự đã xảy ra. Từ 'nếu' đang gây thiệt hại phi thường trong câu đó: nó định khung lại tổn hại như là một khả năng thay vì một thực tế, và nó chuyển trách nhiệm sang nhận thức của người bị tổn thương. Đây không phải là lời xin lỗi — đó là một lời buộc tội ẩn. Và một lời buộc tội ẩn, được đưa ra dưới dạng lời xin lỗi, làm trầm trọng thêm tổn thương ban đầu thay vì giải quyết nó.

Công nhận, bất kỳ nỗ lực liên lạc nào sau mâu thuẫn cũng đáng giá hơn sự im lặng vĩnh viễn trong nhiều trường hợp. Một lời xin lỗi vụng về vẫn có thể báo hiệu rằng mối quan hệ có giá trị, và có những tình huống mà một nỗ lực hàn gắn không hoàn hảo quan trọng hơn việc diễn đạt có chính xác không. Không phải ai cũng có vốn từ cảm xúc để diễn đạt sự hối hận một cách rõ ràng, và sẽ là khắt khe khi đòi hỏi sự chính xác từ ai đó đang cố gắng hết sức với công cụ hạn chế.

Dẫu vậy, lời xin lỗi kèm quá nhiều điều kiện — 'Tôi xin lỗi, nhưng bạn đã khiêu khích tôi', hay 'Tôi xin lỗi nếu lời nói của tôi bị hiểu sai nghĩa' — không chỉ đơn giản là thiếu chính xác. Nó đổ lỗi cho người nhận ngay trong hành động giả vờ nhận trách nhiệm. Nghiên cứu về giao tiếp giữa các cá nhân liên tục cho thấy rằng lời xin lỗi có điều kiện được nhìn nhận là tệ hơn không xin lỗi gì cả bởi những người nhận chúng: họ cảm thấy không chỉ chưa được hàn gắn mà còn chủ động bị bác bỏ. Tổn thương được xác nhận, và sau đó ngay lập tức bị thu nhỏ.

Điều được yêu cầu từ một lời xin lỗi thực sự không phải là ngôn ngữ phức tạp mà là sự sẵn sàng đơn giản để nhận trách nhiệm về một hành động cụ thể. 'Tôi đã nói điều gì đó làm đau bạn, và tôi hối tiếc' không tốn gì và khôi phục điều kiện cơ bản cho niềm tin: sự thừa nhận rằng trải nghiệm của người kia là có thật. Một lời xin lỗi giả tạo chính xác là từ chối sự thừa nhận đó, và khi làm vậy, nó thực hiện, bằng ngôn ngữ trau chuốt, chính xác cùng một sự xóa bỏ như không nói gì cả — ngoại trừ việc nó còn yêu cầu người bị tổn thương phải ngồi nghe hết.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to expose hidden meaning?
options: What a statement like 'I'm sorry if anyone was offended' communicates is not remorse but a refusal to acknowledge.
It is what communicates not remorse a refusal statement sorry if offended.
The statement what communicates is not remorse but a refusal acknowledging.
What communicates the statement is refusal not remorse a acknowledgement.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What X communicates is not Y but Z' delays the key conclusion until the end for maximum emphasis.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … communicates is …' with the key contrast at the end.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive sentence correctly foregrounds what is being evaluated?
options: People perceive conditional apologies as worse than no apology.
Conditional apologies are perceived as worse than no apology at all by the people who receive them.
Conditional apologies perceive worse than no apology people who receive.
It is perceived that conditional apologies worse than no apology people.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'are perceived as' uses the passive to focus on the apologies (subject) rather than on the recipients who evaluate them.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option puts 'conditional apologies' in subject position using a passive form?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence introduces a concession most naturally?
options: Granted, any attempt to reach out after a conflict is preferable to permanent silence in many cases.
Even though granted silence any attempt is preferable conflict.
Of course but not silence attempt conflict preferable.
Conceding which attempt conflict preferable to silence is granted.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' cleanly admits the opposing view before the writer returns to their main argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Find the single-word opener that most naturally admits the other side.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the grammatical function of 'if' in 'I'm sorry if anyone was offended'?
options: It introduces a time clause meaning 'whenever'
It introduces a condition, making the harm hypothetical rather than real
It functions as a contrast marker similar to 'although'
It introduces a result clause
mcQuestions.explanation: 'If' makes the harm conditional and therefore hypothetical — the essay argues this reframes a real injury as a mere possibility.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what 'if' does to the certainty of the statement.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'compound' correctly in its metaphorical sense?
options: An implicit accusation delivered as an apology compounds the original injury.
An implicit accusation compounds being original injury delivered apology.
Compounding the accusation original injury apology delivered implicit.
The original injury compounds an implicit accusation it delivers apology.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Compound an injury/problem' means to make it worse; the subject (accusation) acts on the object (original injury).
mcQuestions.hint: Which option has 'compound' as the main verb with the correct subject and object?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which word means 'a feeling of deep regret or guilt for having done something wrong'?
options: accountability
remorse
acknowledgement
dismissal
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Remorse' is the specific emotional term for deep regret about one's own actions.
mcQuestions.hint: Which word is specifically about a feeling, not an action or process?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase best means 'to accept responsibility for a specific action'?
options: to reframe a specific act
to take ownership of a specific act
to deflect a specific act
to minimise a specific act
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Take ownership of' is the standard collocation for accepting personal responsibility.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase means you are claiming the act as yours and accepting its consequences?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'shifts blame onto' correctly?
options: The over-qualified apology shifts blame onto the recipient in the very act of pretending to take responsibility.
Blame shifts the over-qualified apology onto recipient very act responsibility.
The over-qualified blame apology shifts recipient act onto pretending.
Blame the over-qualified apology onto recipient pretending shifts.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Shifts blame onto + person' is the correct collocation meaning the responsibility is moved to someone else.
mcQuestions.hint: Subject + 'shifts blame onto' + object is the correct structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: In conclusion,
On the contrary,
Even so, the over-qualified apology is not simply imprecise.
Anyway, therefore,
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' acknowledges the concession and pivots back with a stronger claim.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option both acknowledges what was just granted and pivots back clearly?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best opens a paragraph with a concrete, specific description — not a generic claim?
options: Apologies are very important in all human relationships.
There is a particular kind of statement — grammatically an apology, functionally an insult — that has become standard in political speeches and corporate press releases.
Throughout history, people have always struggled to apologise.
It is important to note that some apologies are bad.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B defines the specific type being discussed, names where it appears, and sets up the essay's argument without generic framing.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option names the exact type, where it appears, and creates immediate intrigue?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'implicit accusation' mean in the context of this essay?
options: A formal charge made in a legal document
A direct statement blaming someone
A hidden or unstated blame embedded in another message
An exaggerated criticism
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Implicit' means not directly stated; in the essay, the 'if' in the non-apology hides a suggestion that the injured person may have imagined the harm.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Implicit' means not openly said — what is being blamed without naming it directly?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'not only … but …' correctly?
options: They feel not only unrepaired but actively dismissed.
They feel not only unrepaired but they dismiss actively also.
Not only they feel unrepaired but active dismissal also.
They not only feel unrepaired actively but dismissed.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Not only … but …' requires parallel adjectives. 'Unrepaired' and 'dismissed' are both past participles used as adjectives.
mcQuestions.hint: Both elements after 'not only' and 'but' must be grammatically parallel.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which collocation is correct for describing a corporate announcement designed to manage public opinion?
options: corporate PR
corporate speaking
corporate feeling
corporate claim
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Corporate PR' (public relations) is the standard collocation for a company's official communications designed to shape its public image.
mcQuestions.hint: Which two-word phrase names the practice of managing a company's public image?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option maintains formal register throughout?
options: Non-apologies are basically just mega rude and everyone hates them lol.
A non-apology withholds precisely that acknowledgement, and in doing so performs exactly the same erasure as saying nothing at all.
Like, saying sorry if you feel bad is not actually an apology right??
Non-apologies r worse than silence that's for sure everyone knows this.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B uses precise academic vocabulary ('withholds', 'acknowledgement', 'erasure') in a formal syntactic structure.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid slang, abbreviations, and rhetorical questions with informal markers.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'the passive construction does its work invisibly' mean?
options: The grammar teacher is not explaining passive voice clearly
Using the passive voice in a sentence hides who is responsible for the action
The speaker is very quiet when they speak
The apology is given in a private message rather than publicly
mcQuestions.explanation: The passive construction removes the agent — in 'if anyone was offended', nobody is named as the offender, which is precisely how responsibility is evaded.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what the passive voice hides: who did the action.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what is the minimum a genuine apology must provide?
options: Elaborate and poetic language showing deep emotion
A promise to compensate the injured person financially
The acknowledgement that the other person's experience was real
A detailed explanation of why the harm was caused
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states: 'What is being asked of a genuine apology is … a simple willingness to take ownership … the acknowledgement that the other person's experience was real.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the final paragraph's description of what 'a genuine apology' requires.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Have you ever received an apology that made things worse? Looking back, what exact words or structure made it feel less like an apology and more like an accusation?
